questionText: The heights, $X$ cm, of adult women in a population are modelled by $X \sim N(168, 7^2)$. (a) Find the $90$th percentile of the heights. (b) Find the interquartile range of the heights. Give your answers to $3$ significant figures.

steps.explanation: Heights are normal with mean $\mu = 168$ cm and standard deviation $\sigma = 7$ cm. Part (a) asks for the $90$th percentile and part (b) for the interquartile range.
The $90$th percentile is the height below which $90\%$ of women fall, so the cumulative probability up to it is $0.90$.
The standardised value with $90\%$ of the area below it is $1.282$; it is positive because the $90$th percentile lies above the mean.
Reversing the standardisation with $p_{90} = \mu + z\sigma$ turns the $z$-value back into centimetres.
So $90\%$ of women are shorter than about $177$ cm; equivalently, the tallest $10\%$ are above this height.

steps.description: Find the z-values for the quartiles
steps.explanation: For part (b), the lower and upper quartiles standardise to $\mp 0.6745$, symmetric about the mean because the normal curve is symmetric.

steps.description: Express the interquartile range using the quartiles
steps.explanation: Subtracting the lower quartile from the upper quartile, the mean cancels, leaving twice the distance from the mean to a quartile.
The IQR is therefore $2z\sigma$ with $z = 0.6745$ and $\sigma = 7$; the mean $168$ plays no part in the spread.
This gives an interquartile range of about $9.44$ cm, describing the spread of the middle half of women's heights.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: (a) The $90$th percentile is about $177$ cm. (b) The interquartile range is about $9.44$ cm (3 s.f.).
